subject: Physics
q: For particle P revolving round the centre O with radius of circular path r and regular velocity ω, as shown in below figure, the projection of OP on the x-axis at time t is
question: For particle P revolving round the centre O with radius of circular path r and regular velocity ω, as shown in below figure, the projection of OP on the x-axis at time t is
solution:
As can be seen from the diagram, the initial phase of the particle is given by

φ=30°= π6

The equation of motion of the particle at its initial position at time t=0 can be written as

xt=rcosθ   ...(1)

where, θ is the angular displacement of the particle at time t=t.

Since, the position of the particle at a later instant of time always lags behind its initial position at time t=0, the equation of motion of the particle at any instant of time is given by

 xt=rcosθ+φ   ...2

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (2) to obtain the required expression

x(t)=r cosωt+π6

q: For a simple pendulum, a graph is plotted between its kinetic energy (K.E.) and potential energy (P.E.) against its displacement d. which one of the following represents these correctly? (graphs are schematic and not drawn to scale)
question: For a simple pendulum, a graph is plotted between its kinetic energy (K.E.) and potential energy (P.E.) against its displacement d. which one of the following represents these correctly? (graphs are schematic and not drawn to scale)
solution:
For simple pendulum performing the simple harmonic motion, displacement, y=Asinωtv=dydt=ωAcosωt=ωA2-y2⇒K.E.=12mv2, 
This represents a downward parabola, Y=±A, K.E.=12ω2m A2-A2=0. P.E.=12mω2y2. This represents an upward parabola. On plotting graphs of potential energy & kinetic energy.

subject: Physics
q: The kinetic energy of a simple harmonic oscillator is oscillating with angular frequency of $176 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$. The frequency of this simple harmonic oscillator is $\_\_\_\_$ Hz. $\left[\right.$ take $\left.\pi=\frac{22}{7}\right]$
question: The kinetic energy of a simple harmonic oscillator is oscillating with angular frequency of $176 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$. The frequency of this simple harmonic oscillator is $\_\_\_\_$ Hz. $\left[\right.$ take $\left.\pi=\frac{22}{7}\right]$
solution:
In a simple harmonic oscillator, if the displacement is $x = A \sin(\omega t + \phi)$, the velocity is $v = A\omega \cos(\omega t + \phi)$.
The kinetic energy is given by $K = \frac{1}{2}mv^2 = \frac{1}{2}m A^2 \omega^2 \cos^2(\omega t + \phi)$.
Using the identity $\cos^2 \theta = \frac{1 + \cos 2\theta}{2}$, we get $K = \frac{1}{4}m A^2 \omega^2 [1 + \cos(2\omega t + 2\phi)]$.
The angular frequency of the kinetic energy oscillation is $\omega_{KE} = 2\omega$, where $\omega$ is the angular frequency of the SHM.
Given $\omega_{KE} = 176$ rad/s, we have $2\omega = 176$, which gives $\omega = 88$ rad/s.
The frequency of the simple harmonic oscillator is $f = \frac{\omega}{2\pi}$.
Substituting the values: $f = \frac{88}{2 \times \frac{22}{7}} = \frac{88 \times 7}{44} = 2 \times 7 = 14$ Hz.

subject: Physics
q: The frequency of oscillation of a mass $m$ suspended by a spring is $v_1$. If the length of the spring is cut to half, the same mass oscillates with frequency $v_2$. The value of $v_2/v_1$ is ________.
question: The frequency of oscillation of a mass $m$ suspended by a spring is $v_1$. If the length of the spring is cut to half, the same mass oscillates with frequency $v_2$. The value of $v_2/v_1$ is ________.
solution:
The frequency of oscillation of a spring-mass system is given by $v = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$, where $k$ is the spring constant and $m$ is the mass.

The spring constant $k$ is inversely proportional to the length of the spring $L$, so $k \propto \dfrac{1}{L}$.

When the length of the spring is cut to half, the new length is $L' = \dfrac{L}{2}$. The new spring constant becomes $k' = 2k$.

The new frequency of oscillation is $v_2 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{k'}{m}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{2k}{m}} = \sqrt{2} v_1$.

Therefore, the ratio $\dfrac{v_2}{v_1} = \sqrt{2}$.

Answer: $\sqrt{2}$

q: A particle executes simple harmonic motion between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>–</mo><mi>A</mi><mo> </mo><mtext>and</mtext><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>+</mo><mi>A</mi></math>. If time taken by particle to go from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo> </mo><mo>=</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>; then time taken by particle in going from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is:
question: A particle executes simple harmonic motion between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>–</mo><mi>A</mi><mo> </mo><mtext>and</mtext><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>+</mo><mi>A</mi></math>. If time taken by particle to go from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo> </mo><mo>=</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>; then time taken by particle in going from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is:
solution:
The position of a particle performing an SHM is given by:

x=A sin(ωt+ϕ)

Since at t=0 s, the particle is at x=0, therefore the initial phase ϕ=0.

At time t=2 s, the particle is at x=A2.

∴ A2=A sin2ω⇒ω=π12 s-1

Therefore, time period

T=2πω=24 s

The total time taken from x=0 to x=A is T4=6 s.

Since the time taken from x=0 to x=A2 is 2 s, therefore the time taken from x=A2 to x=A is 4 s.

q: Which of the following expressions corresponds to simple harmonic motion along a straight line, where $\mathrm{x}$ is the displacement and $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}$ are positive constants?
question: Which of the following expressions corresponds to simple harmonic motion along a straight line, where $\mathrm{x}$ is the displacement and $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}$ are positive constants?
solution:
In linear S.H.M., the restoring force acting on particle should always be proportional to the displacement of the particle and directed towards the equilibrium position. i.e., $\mathrm{F} \propto \mathrm{x}$
or $\mathrm{F}=-\mathrm{bx}$ where $\mathrm{b}$ is a positive constant.

q:
The amplitude and phase of a wave that is formed by the superposition of two harmonic travelling waves, $\mathrm{y}_1(\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{t})=4 \sin (\mathrm{kx}-\omega \mathrm{t})$ and $\mathrm{y}_2(\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{t})=2 \sin \left(\mathrm{kx}-\omega \mathrm{t}+\frac{2 \pi}{3}\right)$, are $:$
(Take the angular frequency of initial waves same as $\omega$)
question:
The amplitude and phase of a wave that is formed by the superposition of two harmonic travelling waves, $\mathrm{y}_1(\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{t})=4 \sin (\mathrm{kx}-\omega \mathrm{t})$ and $\mathrm{y}_2(\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{t})=2 \sin \left(\mathrm{kx}-\omega \mathrm{t}+\frac{2 \pi}{3}\right)$, are $:$
(Take the angular frequency of initial waves same as $\omega$)
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \begin{array}{l}\mathrm{A}=\sqrt{2^2+4^2+2 \times 2 \times 4 \times \cos 120^{\circ}} \\ \quad=\sqrt{12}=2 \sqrt{3} \\ \tan \phi=\frac{2 \sin 120^{\circ}}{4+2 \cos 120^{\circ}}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \\ \phi=\frac{\pi}{6}\end{array}\end{aligned}$

solution:
Let x be the displacement at which its kinetic energy will be 25% more than the potential energy.

Given here, A=3 cm and KE=PE+PE4

So, KE=54PE

⇒12mω2A2-x2=54×12mω2x2

⇒A2-x2=54x2

⇒9x24=A2

⇒x=23A

∴ x=23×3 cm

⇒x=2 cm

subject: Physics
q: Two particles $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ of equal masses are suspended from two massless springs of spring constant $\mathrm{k}_1$ and $\mathrm{k}_2$, respectively. If the maximum velocities, during oscillation, are equal, the ratio of amplitude of $A$ and $B$ is
question: Two particles $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ of equal masses are suspended from two massless springs of spring constant $\mathrm{k}_1$ and $\mathrm{k}_2$, respectively. If the maximum velocities, during oscillation, are equal, the ratio of amplitude of $A$ and $B$ is
solution:
$\mathrm{V}_{\max }=\omega \mathrm{A}=\mathrm{m} \omega^2 \mathrm{I}=\mathrm{k}$
$\Rightarrow \omega^2=\frac{\mathrm{k}}{\mathrm{m}} \Rightarrow \omega=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{k}}{\mathrm{m}}} \quad \Rightarrow \omega \propto \sqrt{\mathrm{K}} \quad$ or $\quad \frac{\omega_1}{\omega_2}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{k}_1}{\mathrm{k}_2}}$
$\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{A}} \max =\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{B}} \max \quad \Rightarrow\left(\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{k}_1}{\mathrm{~m}}}\right)\left(\mathrm{A}_1\right)=\left(\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{k}_2}{\mathrm{~m}}}\right)\left(\mathrm{A}_2\right) \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{A}_1}{\mathrm{~A}_2}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{k}_2}{\mathrm{k}_1}}$

question:
An ideal gas enclosed in a vertical cylindrical container supports a freely moving piston of mass M. The piston and the cylinder have equal cross-sectional area A. When the piston is in equilibrium, the volume of the gas is V0 and its pressure is M0. The piston is slightly displaced from the equilibrium position and released. Assuming that the system is completely isolated from its surrounding, the piston executes a simple harmonic motion with frequency
[Assume the system is in space.]
solution:
For an adiabatic process,

Pvγ=constant

Differentiating with respect to v,

dPdvvγ+Pγvγ-1=0

dP=-γPvdv=-γPvAdx



Thus, the acceleration, a=dFM=γPA2MVdx

Comparing with ,  a=-ω2dx, we get,

ω=γP0A2MV0=2πf


f=12πγP0A2MV0

subject: Physics
q: A particle is executing simple harmonic motion. Its amplitude is $A$ and time period is $5$ sec. The time required by it to move from $x = A$ to $x = \dfrac{A}{\sqrt{2}}$ is _______ sec.
question: A particle is executing simple harmonic motion. Its amplitude is $A$ and time period is $5$ sec. The time required by it to move from $x = A$ to $x = \dfrac{A}{\sqrt{2}}$ is _______ sec.
solution:
The equation of simple harmonic motion for a particle starting from the extreme position $x = A$ is given by:

$x = A \cos(\omega t)$

We need to find the time $t$ when the particle reaches $x = \dfrac{A}{\sqrt{2}}$. Substituting this value into the equation:

$\dfrac{A}{\sqrt{2}} = A \cos(\omega t)$

$\cos(\omega t) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$

$\omega t = \dfrac{\pi}{4}$

Since the angular frequency $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi}{T}$, we have:

$\dfrac{2\pi}{T} t = \dfrac{\pi}{4}$

$t = \dfrac{T}{8}$

Given the time period $T = 5$ sec, the required time is:

$t = \dfrac{5}{8}$ sec

Answer: $5/8$

subject: Physics
q: A spring stretches by $2$ mm when it is loaded with a mass of $200$ g. From equilibrium position the mass is further pulled down by $2$ mm and released. The frequency associated with the system and maximum energy in the spring are __________ Hz and __________ J, respectively. (Take g $= 10$ m/s$^2$)
question: A spring stretches by $2$ mm when it is loaded with a mass of $200$ g. From equilibrium position the mass is further pulled down by $2$ mm and released. The frequency associated with the system and maximum energy in the spring are __________ Hz and __________ J, respectively. (Take g $= 10$ m/s$^2$)
solution:
At equilibrium, the restoring force of the spring balances the weight of the mass:

$mg = kx_0$

$k = \dfrac{mg}{x_0} = \dfrac{0.2 \times 10}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 1000$ N/m

The frequency of oscillation is given by:

$f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$

$f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{1000}{0.2}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{5000} = \dfrac{10\sqrt{50}}{2\pi} = \dfrac{5\sqrt{50}}{\pi}$ Hz

The maximum energy in the spring corresponds to its maximum elastic potential energy, which occurs at the maximum extension.

Maximum extension $x_{max} = x_0 + A = 2 \text{ mm} + 2 \text{ mm} = 4 \text{ mm} = 4 \times 10^{-3}$ m

Maximum energy in the spring $U_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2} k x_{max}^2$

$U_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 1000 \times (4 \times 10^{-3})^2$

$U_{max} = 500 \times 16 \times 10^{-6} = 8000 \times 10^{-6} = 8 \times 10^{-3}$ J

Answer: $\dfrac{5\sqrt{50}}{\pi}$ and $8 \times 10^{-3}$

2x2=A2

x=±A2

subject: Physics
q:
A particle undergoing simple harmonic motion has time

dependent displacement given by $x(t)=\mathrm{A} \sin \frac{\pi t}{90}$. The ratio of kinetic to potential energy of this particle at $\mathrm{t}=210 \mathrm{~s}$ will be
question:
A particle undergoing simple harmonic motion has time

dependent displacement given by $x(t)=\mathrm{A} \sin \frac{\pi t}{90}$. The ratio of kinetic to potential energy of this particle at $\mathrm{t}=210 \mathrm{~s}$ will be
solution:
Kinetic energy, $\mathrm{k}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m} \omega^{2} \mathrm{~A}^{2} \cos ^{2} \omega \mathrm{t}$

Potential energy, $U=\frac{1}{2} m \omega^{2} A^{2} \sin ^{2} \omega t$

$\frac{k}{U}=\cot ^{2} \omega t=\cot ^{2} \frac{\pi}{90}(210)=\frac{1}{3}$

subject: Physics
q: In forced oscillation of a particle the amplitude is maximum for a frequency $\omega_1$ of the force, while the energy is maximum for a frequency $\omega_2$ of the force, then
question: In forced oscillation of a particle the amplitude is maximum for a frequency $\omega_1$ of the force, while the energy is maximum for a frequency $\omega_2$ of the force, then
options: $\omega_1 < \omega_2$ when damping is small and $\omega_1>\omega_2$ when damping is large
solution:
Both amplitude and energy get maximised when the frequency is equal to the natural frequency. This is the condition of resonance.
$$
\omega_1=\omega_2
$$

The resultant amplitude is equal to the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt></math> times of amplitude of individual motions. The phase difference between the two motions is _____ (degree)
question: Two waves executing simple harmonic motion travelling in the same direction with same amplitude and frequency are superimposed. The resultant amplitude is equal to the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt></math> times of amplitude of individual motions. The phase difference between the two motions is _____ (degree)
solution:
When two waves of the same frequency superimpose, the resultant amplitude is given by

Aresultant =A12+A22+2A1A2cosϕ

⇒3 A=A2+A2+2A2cosϕ

⇒3A2=2A2+2A2cosϕ

⇒cosϕ=12

∴ϕ=60°

∴Phase difference =60 degree

Given the equation of motion is

y=sinωt+cosωt   ...1

Simplify equation (1) to obtain the standard form:

y=2sinωtcosπ4+cosωtsinπ4=2sinωt+π4   ...2

Comparing equation (2) with standard equation of SHM y=Asinωt+ϕ, we get amplitude of the given oscillation as 2.

subject: Physics
q: In the reported figure, two bodies A and B of masses 200 g and 800 g are attached with the system of springs. Springs are kept in a stretched position with some extension when the system is released. The horizontal surface is assumed to be frictionless. The angular frequency will be _______  rad s-1 when k=20 N m-1.
question: In the reported figure, two bodies A and B of masses 200 g and 800 g are attached with the system of springs. Springs are kept in a stretched position with some extension when the system is released. The horizontal surface is assumed to be frictionless. The angular frequency will be _______  rad s-1 when k=20 N m-1.
solution:
ω=keqμ

μ= reduced mass

Springs are in series connection, 

keq=k1k2k1+k2

keq=k×4k5k=4k5

keq=4×205 N m-1=16 N m-1

μ=m1 m2 m1+m2=0.2×0.80.2+0.8=0.16 kg

ω=160.16=100=10

Their mean position is separated by distance $X_0\left(X_0>A\right)$. If the maximum separation between them is $\left(X_0+A\right)$, the phase difference between their motion is :
question: Two particles are executing simple harmonic motion of the same amplitude $\mathrm{A}$ and frequency $\omega$ along the $x$-axis. Their mean position is separated by distance $X_0\left(X_0>A\right)$. If the maximum separation between them is $\left(X_0+A\right)$, the phase difference between their motion is :
solution: $\phi_1=0$ $\phi_2=\frac{\pi}{2}$

subject: Physics
q: A particle performs simple harmonic motion with amplitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>. Its speed is increased to three times at an instant when its displacement is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>A</mi></mrow><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math>. The new amplitude of motion is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi>n</mi><mi>A</mi></mrow><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> is _____.
question: A particle performs simple harmonic motion with amplitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>. Its speed is increased to three times at an instant when its displacement is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>A</mi></mrow><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math>. The new amplitude of motion is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi>n</mi><mi>A</mi></mrow><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> is _____.
solution:
Speed in SHM in terms of displacement is given by, v=ωA2-x2.

at x=2A3, we get

v=ωA2-2A32=5Aω3

Let the new amplitude be=A'

Then, 

v'=3v⇒ωA'2-2A32=5Aω

⇒A'=73A

Therefore, n=7.

subject: Physics
q:
Two blocks of masses $m$ and $M,(M \gt m)$, are placed on a frictionless table as shown in figure. A massless spring with spring constant k is attached with the lower block. If the system is slightly displaced and released then
($\mu=$ coefficient of friction between the two blocks)

(A) The time period of small oscillation of the two blocks is $\mathrm{T}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{(\mathrm{~m}+\mathrm{M})}{\mathrm{k}}}$
(B) The acceleration of the blocks is $\mathrm{a}=\frac{\mathrm{kx}}{\mathrm{M}+\mathrm{m}}$ ($\mathrm{x}=$ displacement of the blocks from the mean position)
(C) The magnitude of the frictional force on the upper block is $\frac{m \mu|x|}{M+m}$
(D) The maximum amplitude of the upper block, if it does not slip, is $\frac{\mu(M+m) g}{k}$
(E) Maximum frictional force can be $\mu(\mathrm{M}+\mathrm{m}) \mathrm{g}$.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Two blocks of masses $m$ and $M,(M \gt m)$, are placed on a frictionless table as shown in figure. A massless spring with spring constant k is attached with the lower block. If the system is slightly displaced and released then
($\mu=$ coefficient of friction between the two blocks)

(A) The time period of small oscillation of the two blocks is $\mathrm{T}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{(\mathrm{~m}+\mathrm{M})}{\mathrm{k}}}$
(B) The acceleration of the blocks is $\mathrm{a}=\frac{\mathrm{kx}}{\mathrm{M}+\mathrm{m}}$ ($\mathrm{x}=$ displacement of the blocks from the mean position)
(C) The magnitude of the frictional force on the upper block is $\frac{m \mu|x|}{M+m}$
(D) The maximum amplitude of the upper block, if it does not slip, is $\frac{\mu(M+m) g}{k}$
(E) Maximum frictional force can be $\mu(\mathrm{M}+\mathrm{m}) \mathrm{g}$.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
(A) As both blocks moving together so Time period $=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{~m}}{\mathrm{~K}}} ;$ where $\mathrm{m}=\mathrm{M}+\mathrm{m}$
$\mathrm{T}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{M}+\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{~K}}}$
(B) Let block is displaced by x in $(+\mathrm{ve})$ direction so force on block will be in(-ve) direction
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{F}=-\mathrm{Kx} \\ & (\mathrm{M}+\mathrm{m}) \mathrm{a}=-\mathrm{Kx} \\ & \mathrm{a}=-\frac{\mathrm{Kx}}{(\mathrm{M}+\mathrm{m})}
\end{aligned}$
(C) As upper block is moving due to friction thus
$\mathrm{f}=\mathrm{ma}=\frac{\mathrm{mKx}}{(\mathrm{M}+\mathrm{m})}$
(D) This option is like two block problem in friction for maximum amplitude, force on block is also maximum, for which both blocks are moving together.

$\begin{aligned}
& K A=(M+m) a \\ & a=\frac{K A}{(M+m)} \\ & f=m a=\frac{m K A}{(M+m)} \\ & f_{\max }=f_L=\mu \mathrm{mg} \\ & f=\mu m g \\ & \frac{m K A}{(M+m)}=\mu m g \\ & A=\frac{\mu(M+m) g}{K}
\end{aligned}$
(E) Maximum friction can be $\mu \mathrm{mg}$ as force is acting between blocks \& normal force here is mg.

subject: Physics
q: Two springs, of force constants $k_1$ and $k_2$, are connected to a mass $\mathrm{m}$ as shown. The frequency of oscillation of the mass is $\mathrm{f}$. If both $k_1$ and $k_2$ are made four times their original values, the frequency of oscillation becomes
question: Two springs, of force constants $k_1$ and $k_2$, are connected to a mass $\mathrm{m}$ as shown. The frequency of oscillation of the mass is $\mathrm{f}$. If both $k_1$ and $k_2$ are made four times their original values, the frequency of oscillation becomes
solution: $\begin{aligned} f & =\frac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\frac{k_1+k_2}{m}} \\ f^{\prime} & =\frac{1}{2 \pi} 2 \sqrt{\frac{k_1+k_2}{m}}=2 f\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A simple pendulum with length 100 cm and bob of mass 250 g is executing S.H.M of amplitude 10 cm. The maximum tension in the string is found to be x40 N. The value of x is _____.
question: A simple pendulum with length 100 cm and bob of mass 250 g is executing S.H.M of amplitude 10 cm. The maximum tension in the string is found to be x40 N. The value of x is _____.
solution:
From the given figure we can see that 

sinθ0=Al=10100=110

From conservation of energy mv22=mgl(1-cosθ0)

Maximum tension occurs at mean position

T=mg+mv2l⇒T=mg+2mg(1-cosθ0)

Substituting the values,

T=mg(1+2(1-1-sin2θ0)⇒T=mg(3-21-1100)⇒T=0.25×9.8(3-2(1-1200))⇒T=0.25×9.8×1.01=2.4745 N

It is given that

 T=x40⇒x=40×2.4745=98.98≈99

subject: Physics
q: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> and charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi></math> is connected to a spring of spring constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi></math>. It is oscillating along <math><mi>x</mi></math>-direction about its equilibrium position in the horizontal plane, taken to be at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, with an amplitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>. An electric field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math> is applied along the <math><mi>x</mi></math>-direction. Which of the following statements is correct?
question: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> and charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi></math> is connected to a spring of spring constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi></math>. It is oscillating along <math><mi>x</mi></math>-direction about its equilibrium position in the horizontal plane, taken to be at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, with an amplitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>. An electric field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math> is applied along the <math><mi>x</mi></math>-direction. Which of the following statements is correct?
options: The total energy of the system is  <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac><mi>m</mi><msup><mrow><mi>ω</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac><mfrac><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>q</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow></mfrac></math>

The new equilibrium position is at a distance  <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>q</mi><mi>E</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow></mfrac></math>  from  <math><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mi> </mi><mn>0</mn></math>

The new equilibrium position is at a distance  <math><mfrac><mrow><mi>q</mi><mi>E</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>k</mi></mrow></mfrac></math>  from  <math><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mi> </mi><mn>0</mn></math>

The total energy of the system is  <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac><mi>m</mi><msup><mrow><mi>ω</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac><mfrac><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>q</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow></mfrac></math> .
solution: The equilibrium position will shift to a point where the resultant force is equal to zero. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi><msub><mi>x</mi><mrow><mi>e</mi><mi>q</mi></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mi>q</mi><mi>E</mi><mi> </mi><mi> </mi><mo>⇒</mo><msub><mi>x</mi><mrow><mi>e</mi><mi>q</mi></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>q</mi><mi>E</mi></mrow><mi>k</mi></mfrac></math>. Energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mi>m</mi><msup><mi>ω</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mfenced close="]" open="[" separators="|"><mrow><msup><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mfenced separators="|"><mfrac><mrow><mi>q</mi><mi>E</mi></mrow><mi>k</mi></mfrac></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mi>m</mi><msup><mi>ω</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mfrac><mrow><msup><mi>q</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>E</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow><mi>k</mi></mfrac></math>.

subject: Physics
q: When a particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is attached to a vertical spring of spring constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi></math> and released, its motion, is described by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi><mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><msup><mi>sin</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>ωt</mi></math>, where '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></math>' is measured from the lower end of upstretched spring. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">ω</mi></math> is :
question: When a particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is attached to a vertical spring of spring constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi></math> and released, its motion, is described by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi><mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><msup><mi>sin</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>ωt</mi></math>, where '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></math>' is measured from the lower end of upstretched spring. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">ω</mi></math> is :
solution:
y=y0sin2ωt

y=y02(1-cot2ωt)

y-y02=-y02 cos2ωt

Y=A cos2ωt

2ω=km

maximum displacemnet =y0=mgk

y0×(2ω)2=2g

ω=g2y0

q: Two charges, each equal to <math> <mrow> <mtext>q</mtext> </mrow> </math>, are kept at <math> <mrow> <mtext>x</mtext> <mo>=</mo> <mo>-</mo> <mtext>a</mtext> </mrow> </math> and <math> <mrow> <mtext>x</mtext> <mo> = </mo> <mtext>a</mtext> </mrow> </math> on the x-axis. A particle of mass <math> <mrow> <mtext>m</mtext> </mrow> </math> and charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mtext>q</mtext><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mtext>q</mtext><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math>  is placed at the origin. If charge <math> <mrow> <msub> <mtext>q</mtext> <mn>0</mn> </msub> </mrow> </math> is given a small displacement <math> <mrow> <mtext>(y << a)</mtext> </mrow> </math> along the y-axis, the net force acting on the particle is proportional to :
question: Two charges, each equal to <math> <mrow> <mtext>q</mtext> </mrow> </math>, are kept at <math> <mrow> <mtext>x</mtext> <mo>=</mo> <mo>-</mo> <mtext>a</mtext> </mrow> </math> and <math> <mrow> <mtext>x</mtext> <mo> = </mo> <mtext>a</mtext> </mrow> </math> on the x-axis. A particle of mass <math> <mrow> <mtext>m</mtext> </mrow> </math> and charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mtext>q</mtext><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mtext>q</mtext><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math>  is placed at the origin. If charge <math> <mrow> <msub> <mtext>q</mtext> <mn>0</mn> </msub> </mrow> </math> is given a small displacement <math> <mrow> <mtext>(y << a)</mtext> </mrow> </math> along the y-axis, the net force acting on the particle is proportional to :
solution:
⇒ F net = 2Fcos θ

Fnet=2kqq2y2+a22yy2+a2

Fnet=2kqq2yy2+a23/2≈kq2a3y⇒F∝-y

Net force F is opposite the direction of displacement y. Hence, negative sign is used.

q: A cylindrical block of wood (density = 650 kg m-3), of base area 30 cm2 and height 54 cm, floats in a liquid of density 900 kg m-3. The block is depressed slightly and then released. The time period of the resulting oscillations of the block would be equal to that of a simple pendulum of length (nearly) :
question: A cylindrical block of wood (density = 650 kg m-3), of base area 30 cm2 and height 54 cm, floats in a liquid of density 900 kg m-3. The block is depressed slightly and then released. The time period of the resulting oscillations of the block would be equal to that of a simple pendulum of length (nearly) :
solution:
Case-I

In equilibrium

Fup=mg

ViρLg=mg

AhρLg=ALρsg………1



Case-II

Let block is pressed by x more in liquid

Fnet=Fup1-mg

=Vi1ρLg-mg

Fnet=A h+xρLg-ALρsg

Fnet=AhρLg+AxρLg-ALρsg. . . . . . . . . . (2)

from equation (1) & (2)

Fnet=AxρLg restoring force in upward direction

α=Fnetm=AρLgAρsLx

α=ω2x for SHM

and ω2=gl

so gl=ρLgρsL

so l=ρsLρL=650×54900=39cm

subject: Physics
q: A particle is executing simple harmonic motion with time period 2 s and amplitude 1 cm . If D and d are the total distance and displacement covered by the particle in 12.5 s , then $\frac{\mathrm{D}}{\mathrm{d}}$ is
question: A particle is executing simple harmonic motion with time period 2 s and amplitude 1 cm . If D and d are the total distance and displacement covered by the particle in 12.5 s , then $\frac{\mathrm{D}}{\mathrm{d}}$ is
solution:
A = 1 cm
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{n}=\frac{12.5}{2}=6.25 \text { cycles } \\ & \therefore \mathrm{D}=4 \times 6+1=25 \\ & \mathrm{~d}=1 \\ & \frac{D}{d}=25\end{aligned}$
